title: Contains Duplicate
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: No single algorithm is both asymptotically fastest (O(n)) and minimal memory (O(1)) for this problem. solution.cpp is the recommended balance (runtime-first, O(1) extra memory): at n <= 1e5 an in-place sort ties the hash set in practical runtime while needing no auxiliary table. The two extremes are kept as documented variants. See notes.md.

variants.languageReason: C++ std::sort is introsort with an inlined comparator, so it is markedly faster than C's qsort (which calls the comparator through a function pointer) while still sorting in place. Native, no GC or interpreter startup.
variants.approachJustification: In-place std::sort followed by an adjacent-equality scan. O(1) extra memory; practical runtime ties the hash set at n <= 1e5.

variants.languageReason: Native, no GC or interpreter startup, with the control to build a flat open-addressing hash set whose contiguous storage is far more cache-friendly than std::unordered_set's node-based layout. Asymptotically O(n) and wins decisively when a duplicate appears early (early exit).
variants.approachJustification: Open-addressing hash set (linear probing, load factor <= 0.5) with a Murmur3-style finalizer; returns on the first collision.

variants.languageReason: Smallest runtime/stdlib baseline among the allowed languages, which minimizes the reported memory footprint. Manual control allows an in-place sort with zero auxiliary allocation. Slower than std::sort because qsort calls its comparator through a function pointer.
variants.approachJustification: In-place qsort followed by an adjacent-equality scan, using an overflow-safe comparator.

languageReason: See variants[].languageReason. Recommended default is solution.cpp (C++ in-place sort): best practical runtime/memory balance for this problem.
reasoningSummary: Detecting any duplicate is either 'remember what you have seen' (hashing: spend memory to gain asymptotic speed) or 'bring equal values next to each other' (sorting: spend a log factor of time to keep memory at O(1)). The value range (-1e9..1e9) rules out a counting array. At n <= 1e5 the two are a practical runtime tie, so the in-place sort is the recommended fast-and-lean default.
approachJustification: Ship a recommended balanced default (in-place sort) plus the two Pareto endpoints: a flat hash set for asymptotic/early-exit speed and a C in-place sort for the absolute-minimum memory baseline.
performanceStrategy: Recommended: in-place std::sort (inlined introsort) + adjacent scan, O(1) extra memory. Extremes: flat open-addressing + early exit (asymptotic speed); C qsort baseline (minimum memory).
timeComplexity: O(n log n) recommended / O(n) average (hash) / O(n log n) (C sort)
spaceComplexity: O(1) extra recommended / O(n) (hash) / O(1) extra (C sort)
